summary: I was aware of Pearl Harbor Day and the looming war as any teenager might be. The attack on Pearl Harbor marked the beginning of a conflict that would soon draft me into service. It was a pivotal event that shaped my path into the military during World War II.

When I first entered the service, I went to basic training in the Signal Corps at Camp Van Dorn in Mississippi. It was my initial military experience before moving on to infantry training. The Signal Corps was where I got my start in the Army.

Camp Van Dorn in Mississippi was where I did my basic training after being drafted in September 1943. It was my first time away from home and an eye-opening experience meeting people from all over the country. The camp was where I learned the esprit de corps and patriotism that bonded us.

Before entering the service, I had just graduated from high school and was attending Loyola University New Orleans in a pre-med program. The draft interrupted my college plans as they started pushing heavily for enlistments. I had initially tried to join the Marines but ended up in the Army Infantry instead.

I initially wanted to enlist in the United States Marine Corps because of their pretty uniforms, but they rejected me due to flat feet. Later, I served alongside Marines in the Pacific, especially at Guam, where we coordinated closely in combat. Despite the initial rejection, I developed respect for the Marines during the war.

After being rejected by the Marines, I was drafted into the United States Army Infantry in September 1943. I was assigned to an anti-tank platoon attached to line companies, which gave me a bit of a buffer from direct frontline combat. Infantry life was tough, involving combat, disease, and harsh conditions, especially in the Pacific theater.

I was assigned to the 77th Infantry Division, which was an illustrious unit that fought exclusively in the Pacific. After basic training and Camp Pickett in Virginia, I joined the 77th and went through jungle training in Hawaii. The division participated in major battles like Guam and Saipan during World War II.

The 77th Infantry Division was split, with half going to Germany and half to the Pacific. I was in the Pacific half, so I didn't serve in Germany, but I was aware that the European front was a major part of the war effort. My focus remained on the Pacific battles.

My combat experience was entirely in the Pacific War, where the 77th Infantry Division fought alongside Marines in places like Saipan, Guam, and the Philippines. The conditions were brutal with jungle warfare, tropical diseases, and constant danger. The Pacific War was a harsh and defining chapter of my military service.

Before going to the Pacific, I had only seen the enemy portrayed in movies, especially those starring Robert Ryan, who depicted the Japanese as cruel people. Those films shaped some of my early fears about what I might face in combat. It was a mix of apprehension and uncertainty before deployment.

After basic training, I was sent to Camp Pickett in Virginia, where replacements for the 77th Infantry Division were processed and assigned. It was there that I learned I would be in the infantry and was placed in an anti-tank platoon. Camp Pickett was a key staging point before heading overseas.

We stopped in Hawaii for jungle training after leaving the West Coast, passing under the Golden Gate Bridge, which was a beautiful sight. Hawaii was where I received specialized training for the anti-tank platoon before heading into combat. It was a crucial preparation phase for the Pacific battles ahead.

I remember leaving the West Coast and passing under the Golden Gate Bridge, which was a beautiful and memorable sight. It was my first time seeing something like that, and it marked the beginning of my journey into the Pacific War. The moment was filled with both awe and trepidation.

Phil Hensley was my closest buddy during the war, and we were part of a small group of about six men who stayed close throughout our service. Even now, decades later, we have tried to keep in touch, though only one sergeant and I remain. Phil was a key part of my wartime experience and camaraderie.

We were initially slated to land in Saipan but were redirected to the Philippines and Guam as the situation evolved. The Philippines was part of the Pacific campaign where the 77th Infantry Division was active. My service included time in the Philippines as part of the broader effort to retake territory from Japanese forces.

We were originally supposed to land in Saipan, but the situation there stabilized, so we were redirected to Guam. Saipan was a major battle in the Pacific War, and though I didn't fight there directly, it was part of the campaign I was involved in. The island was a key strategic point in the Pacific theater.

The Battle of Guam in 1944 was my first combat action, where the 77th Infantry Division fought alongside the Marines. We landed under difficult conditions, circling until early morning before going ashore. The battle was intense, with dead soldiers everywhere, and it was a harsh introduction to war.

During the Battle of Guam and other operations, we worked closely with the United States Marine Corps, sharing resources like tanks and flamethrowers when needed. The Marines had their own support units, but cooperation was essential on the ground. Despite initial enlistment rejection, I respected their professionalism and camaraderie.

Malaria was one of the many diseases we faced in the Pacific, along with dengue fever and dysentery. The tropical environment made illness a constant threat, sometimes as dangerous as combat. We had to rely on medications like Atabrine to try to keep these diseases at bay during our campaigns.

Dengue fever was another serious illness that afflicted soldiers in the Pacific, alongside malaria and dysentery. The jungle conditions and constant rain made it easy to contract these diseases. It was a harsh reality of infantry life that added to the challenges of fighting in such a hostile environment.

Atabrine was the medication we used to combat malaria during our time in the Pacific. Running out of Atabrine tablets was a serious concern because malaria was a constant threat. It was part of the daily struggle to stay healthy while facing combat and harsh conditions.

entity: Ours To Hold It High: The History of the 77th Infantry Division in World War II
summary: I am aware of the book 'Ours To Hold It High: The History of the 77th Infantry Division in World War II,' which chronicles the division's actions in the Pacific. It captures the experiences and sacrifices of men like me who served in the 77th. The book is a valuable record of our division's role in the war.
